summary: This brief abstract outlines Nietzsche's critique of the moral disintegration of late 19th-century Germany. It highlights his definition of morality as a doctrine of supremacy relations that shape life, while noting the ambiguity and multiplicity of moralities in his broader work.

principles.statement: Morality is fundamentally about the relations of supremacy that shape the phenomenon of life.
principles.rationale: He viewed morality not as absolute truths, but as power dynamics that dictate how life manifests and develops.
principles.source_quote: the doctrine of the relations of supremacy (Herrschafts-Verhaltnissen) under which the phenomenon of 'life' comes to be.

signature_quotes.text: the doctrine of the relations of supremacy (Herrschafts-Verhaltnissen) under which the phenomenon of 'life' comes to be.

anti_patterns.description: The progressive 'moral' disintegration of late 19th century Germany.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Nietzsche criticized the prevailing moral trends of his time, viewing them as a disintegration of society rather than true moral progress.
